description: Defines key evidence terms — 'Court', 'fact', 'relevant', 'document', 'evidence', 'proved', 'disproved', 'not proved' — that the rest of the Act builds on.

offence: Motive preparation and previous or subsequent conduct
description: Facts showing motive or preparation for any fact in issue, and the conduct of a party or agent in reference to the proceeding, are relevant.

offence: Confession to police officer not to be proved
description: No confession made to a police officer can be proved against a person accused of any offence.

offence: Statements by persons who cannot be called as witnesses
description: Statements of a person who is dead or cannot be found are relevant in stated circumstances, including a statement as to the cause of death (dying declaration).
When the Court has to form an opinion on a point of foreign law, science, art, handwriting, or finger impressions, the opinions of specially skilled persons are relevant.
Oral evidence must in all cases be direct — the witness must have personally perceived the fact (seen, heard, or otherwise sensed) being testified to.
Electronic records are admissible as documents subject to the conditions in this section, including a certificate identifying the record and the device that produced it.
Whoever desires a Court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts must prove that those facts exist.

offence: Court may presume existence of certain facts
description: The Court may presume the existence of any fact which it thinks likely to have happened, regard being had to the common course of natural events, human conduct, and public/private business.
